skill.name: personal-tool-builder
skill.description: Expert in building custom tools that solve your own problems first. The best products often start as personal tools - scratch your own itch, build for yourself, then discover others have the same issue.
skill.summary: Guides you through building personal productivity tools using the scratch-your-own-itch methodology, from ugly scripts to polished products.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The detected patterns (exec, backticks, file operations, SQLite) appear in code examples within documentation, not in executable skill code. This is an instructional skill that teaches tool-building methodologies without performing any actual system operations.

content.user_title: Build Custom Tools That Solve Your Problems
content.value_statement: Stop waiting for the perfect tool to exist. Learn to build your own solutions from real pain points, starting with ugly scripts that work and evolving them into polished products only when proven useful.
actual_capabilities: Guide you through identifying real problems worth solving with custom tools
Help you build CLI tools using Node.js or Python frameworks
Design local-first applications with SQLite or JSON file storage
Apply the 10-minute test to validate tool ideas before building
Structure the evolution from ugly script to shareable product
Teach dogfooding practices to improve tool usability
limitations: Does not provide actual tool execution - only guidance and code examples
Focuses on individual personal tools, not enterprise-scale solutions
Examples use JavaScript and Python - other languages not covered
Does not include cloud sync or multi-user collaboration patterns

use_cases.title: Automate Repetitive Tasks
use_cases.description: Transform manual tasks you do 10x per day into automated scripts that save hours weekly.
use_cases.target_user: Developers tired of repetitive manual workflows

use_cases.title: Build Your Perfect Productivity App
use_cases.description: Create a custom tool tailored to your exact workflow instead of adapting to existing software.
use_cases.target_user: Knowledge workers with unique workflow requirements

use_cases.title: Turn Side Project Into Product
use_cases.description: Evolve a personal script into a polished product that others want to use and pay for.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Your First Tool Idea
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to build a personal tool but I'm not sure what problem to solve. Help me identify 3 repetitive tasks I do regularly that could be automated. Ask me questions about my daily workflow to find the best opportunities.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have general interest in automation but no specific problem in mind

prompt_templates.title: Apply the 10-Minute Test
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm considering building a tool to [describe problem]. Help me apply the 10-minute test: validate that this is a real itch worth scratching, that I experience it weekly, and that I would actually use the solution daily.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a potential tool idea and want to validate it before building

prompt_templates.title: Design a CLI Tool Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to build a CLI tool that [describe functionality]. Recommend the appropriate stack (Node.js or Python), suggest the command structure, and help me write the initial skeleton with proper argument parsing and error handling.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a validated problem and are ready to start building

prompt_templates.title: Evolve Script to Product
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a working script that solves my problem but it's ugly and hardcoded. Help me refactor it into a maintainable tool with proper configuration, documentation, and error handling that could be shared with others.
prompt_templates.scenario: Your script works for you but you want to polish it for broader use

output_examples.input: I do this manual task 10 times per day: copying data from emails into a spreadsheet
output_examples.output: This is a strong itch - frequent, time-consuming, and clearly defined. A personal tool could parse incoming emails and auto-populate your spreadsheet. Start with a simple script that processes one email format, test it for a week, then expand to handle variations.

output_examples.input: Should I build a cross-platform GUI app or a CLI tool for my note-taking system?
output_examples.output: Start with CLI. It's faster to build, easier to modify, and you can always add a GUI later if needed. Most personal tools work fine as CLI. Build the ugliest working version first - you can polish the interface after you've proven you use it daily.

best_practices: Start with the ugliest possible solution that works - perfection kills motivation
Use your tool daily to feel pain points and drive improvements naturally
Only add configuration, documentation, and error handling after the tool proves useful
anti_patterns: Building tools for imaginary users without real feedback from actual usage
Over-engineering with complex architecture before validating the core concept
Skipping dogfooding and missing obvious usability issues that daily use would reveal

faq.question: What if someone already built a tool for my problem?
faq.answer: Existing tools solve generic cases, not your exact workflow. Personal tools have perfect product-market fit for you. You can still use existing tools for reference, but custom tools often work better for specific needs.

faq.question: How do I know when to polish my ugly script?
faq.answer: After using it daily for 2-4 weeks, if you still reach for it automatically and feel annoyed when it breaks, it's proven useful. That's when investing in polish and documentation makes sense.

faq.question: Should I share my personal tools publicly?
faq.answer: Only after they've proven useful to you for at least a month. If others discover it and find value, great. But don't build for an audience - build for yourself first, share only if it feels natural.

faq.question: What programming language should I use?
faq.answer: Use whatever you're most comfortable with. Speed matters more than language choice. JavaScript and Python have good CLI libraries, but the best language is the one where you can build fastest.

faq.question: How do I handle data storage for personal tools?
faq.answer: Start with JSON files in your home directory. Upgrade to SQLite when you need queries or relationships. Avoid cloud storage unless sync is absolutely necessary - local-first means no server costs and works forever.

faq.question: What if I lose motivation to maintain my tool?
faq.answer: That's normal and okay. Personal tools don't need maintenance if they work. If it stops working after months of use, you either fix it because you need it, or you've outgrown the problem. Both are valid outcomes.
